posts.text: Iran's Tasnim news agency says Hormuz will not reopen until Lebanon ceasefire holds, oil waivers issued https://t.co/MBMekFCnGE https://t.co/MBMekFCnGE
posts.summary: Iran's state media has indicated it may restrict passage through the Strait of Hormuz—a critical chokepoint for global oil exports—until a ceasefire is established in Lebanon and oil sanctions waivers are granted. This threat could significantly disrupt global oil supplies and elevate energy prices if implemented.

However, routine military exercises typically have limited lasting impact on energy markets unless they escalate into broader conflict.

posts.text: US VP Vance arrives in Switzerland for peace talks with Iran https://t.co/CWjYcGqk3q https://t.co/CWjYcGqk3q
posts.summary:
# Oil Market Summary

US Vice President Vance's arrival in Switzerland for Iran peace talks could potentially ease geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, which may exert downward pressure on oil prices if negotiations progress toward reducing sanctions or regional conflict risks. Conversely, any breakdown in talks could sustain elevated oil prices due to ongoing Iran-related geopolitical uncertainty.

posts.text: Iranian negotiators heading to Switzerland. US VP JD Vance expected to travel too later today. https://t.co/LsIUMJNyHj
posts.summary: Iranian and US negotiators are expected to meet in Switzerland, potentially signaling diplomatic engagement that could affect crude oil market dynamics given Iran's role as a major oil producer and current sanctions environment. Any progress toward de-escalation could influence global oil supply concerns and pricing.

posts.text: Iran says the Strait of Hormuz is now closed. The US says it remains open and that 17 million barrels of oil crossed the waterway today (that’s higher than pre-war, and a lot higher when the bypass pipelines volumes are added). https://t.co/LbkE3Iq9Ya
posts.summary: Iran claims it has closed the Strait of Hormuz, but U.S. data indicates oil flows through the waterway remain robust at 17 million barrels daily, exceeding pre-conflict levels and suggesting minimal market disruption despite the rhetorical escalation.

posts.text: Iran isn’t losing any time re-starting its oil production and fresh exports (📷 via @CopernicusEU Sentinel-2 🛰️ from earlier today, showing multiple super tankers loading at Iran’s Kharg Island terminal) https://t.co/agqZcLzA5H
posts.summary: Iran has rapidly resumed oil production and exports following recent developments, with satellite imagery confirming multiple supertankers loading at the Kharg Island terminal, potentially increasing global oil supply and affecting market dynamics.

posts.text: Joint Maritime Information Center says oil tankers and other vessels can use the southern (Omani) shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz with “AIS on, radars radiating, running lights on, and normal use of VHF” either at night or day. https://t.co/f7Yyv9SIs5
posts.summary: The Joint Maritime Information Center has clarified that vessels can transit the southern Omani shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz with standard navigation systems operating, suggesting reduced security risks for oil tankers using this route. This guidance may ease concerns about disruptions to global crude shipments through one of the world's most critical chokepoints.

posts.text: Geothermal energy has great advantages as a source of zero-carbon dispatchable power, but conventional projects are limited by needing specific geological conditions. Enhanced geothermal systems (EGS) use the technologies of hydraulic fracturing and horizontal drilling, developed
posts.summary: Enhanced geothermal systems could reduce oil demand for power generation and heating by providing dispatchable zero-carbon energy without geological limitations, potentially impacting long-term oil consumption in sectors traditionally reliant on fossil fuels.

posts.text:
MAP OF THE DAY: Iran has published a new chart showing two shipping lanes in Hormuz to the south of the ones that Tehran unveiled weeks ago. The new lanes are to the north of the ones used before the war. The Omani lane remains in operation too. 

Before (left) and now (right) https://t.co/oDIVgmolgN
posts.summary: Iran has designated new northbound shipping lanes through the Strait of Hormuz that bypass previously established routes, potentially creating alternative pathways for oil transit amid regional tensions. The move underscores ongoing efforts to maintain critical energy supply routes through one of the world's most strategically important chokepoints for global oil markets.

posts.text: Switzerland confirms that the US-Iran (plus Pakistan and Qatar) talks scheduled for today and the weekend had been postponed. The delay comes after strong fighting between Israel and Hezbollah in southern Lebanon.
posts.summary: Diplomatic talks between the US and Iran have been postponed due to escalating Israel-Hezbollah conflict in Lebanon, potentially delaying negotiations over Iranian oil sanctions relief and raising geopolitical risk premiums in crude markets.

# Oil Market Implications

The lifting of Iran's ports blockade could increase Iranian oil exports to global markets, potentially adding supply pressure and weighing on crude prices if volumes ramp up significantly.

posts.text: Saudi Arabia has started moving a cluster of empty  (ballast) VLCC tankers it has kept near India for most of the conflict toward the Gulf of Oman for a potential Strait of Hormuz crossing. Another sign that oil production in the Persian Gulf is/will be coming back. https://t.co/BgrAzxxD46
posts.summary: Saudi Arabia is repositioning empty supertanker vessels stored near India toward the Strait of Hormuz, suggesting a potential increase in Persian Gulf oil exports in the near term. The movement signals confidence that regional oil production disruptions may be easing or that export capacity is being prepared for higher output.

posts.text:
And where Kuwait goes, so it would go the rest of the Persian Gulf region. Iraq will face some more difficulties, but for KSA and UAE, it would be a lot easier. 

This is the @Opinion column I wrote June 8 about the re-start of production in the region. 

https://t.co/prPkYTsSwO
posts.summary: Javier Blas discusses how oil production restarts in the Persian Gulf region would likely affect different countries differently, with Saudi Arabia and the UAE better positioned to resume output than Iraq, while Kuwait's actions could signal trends across the broader region.

posts.text:
MUST READ: Kuwait has started ramping up oil production and plans to reach 2 million b/d within a week (from 0.5m b/d during the war), KPC CEO Sheikh Nawaf Al-Sabah tells @FionamMacDonald in an interview. He says recovery faster than previously thought.
https://t.co/RMethuGdgM
posts.summary: Kuwait is rapidly restoring oil production to 2 million barrels per day within a week, up from 0.5 million during the conflict, suggesting faster-than-expected supply recovery that could ease global oil market tightness. The accelerated production ramp signals potential downward pressure on crude prices if the recovery trajectory continues as planned.

posts.text: Our experts will be attending Intersolar Europe 2026, taking place next week at Messe München in Germany. Across the conference programme, our experts will deliver six presentations and act as a moderator for one, bringing our research, insights and analysis directly to industry
posts.summary: Wood Mackenzie will present research and insights at Intersolar Europe 2026, though the conference's focus on solar energy has limited direct implications for oil markets. The company's participation highlights the energy industry's continued shift toward renewables, which may influence long-term energy transition strategies relevant to oil demand outlooks.

posts.text: The longer the Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted, the higher oil, gas and electricity prices will rise. If US/Iran negotiations continue to make progress, and the strait reopens within a few weeks, the outcome will look most like the Quick Peace scenario that WoodMac set out https://t.co/pAANRp57jA
posts.summary: A prolonged disruption of the Strait of Hormuz would push oil, gas, and electricity prices higher, while a quick reopening through US-Iran negotiations could limit price increases to levels outlined in Wood Mackenzie's Quick Peace scenario.

posts.text:
The Iran war has caused the greatest energy supply shock in living memory. Our Horizons special edition lays out three distinct scenarios for the end of the war and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and the implications of these for oil & gas supply, demand and prices. 
 
The https://t.co/bAMWKiK5E3
posts.summary: Wood Mackenzie has released analysis examining three scenarios for resolving the Iran conflict and reopening the Strait of Hormuz, with potential significant impacts on global oil and gas supply, demand, and pricing. The analysis addresses what the firm characterizes as the largest energy supply disruption in recent history.

posts.text: The US deregulated power market model faces its greatest test. Data centre growth will depend heavily on natural gas, and the historically high cost of building and operating gas plants presents an enormous challenge in the deregulated markets of PJM and ERCOT. To incentivise new https://t.co/A4bReGrjRS
posts.summary:
# Summary

US deregulated power markets face pressure to attract natural gas generation needed for data center demand, but high capital costs for gas plants threaten investment in competitive markets like PJM and ERCOT. This supply constraint could support natural gas prices if alternative incentive mechanisms aren't implemented.
